A fresh furrow cuts through the tall grass, too straight for any plow. The ground trembles once before the ankheg bursts up beneath the trail.
Laughing calls move through grass high enough to hide the pack. The hyenas test the party from several sides before committing.
A milestone leans beside a wagon rut where the grass has been flattened into hiding places. Raiders rise when the party slows to read it.
The wind makes the plains look like rolling water, erasing all but the largest landmarks. Reading cloud shadow and slope keeps the route true.
A crust of dry earth hides a maze of burrows. A quick step avoids a twisted ankle when the ground gives way.
A blue-black thunderhead builds across half the sky, trailing curtains of rain miles away. The air smells of grass, dust, and lightning.
A lone herd bell rings in the grass without an animal in sight. Tracks nearby suggest rustlers, predators, or both.
A thin line of smoke crawls along the horizon where dry grass has caught fire. Stopping it now may save farms beyond the next rise.
A weathered marker has been knocked over in a field of rusted spearheads. Restoring it reveals a name someone recently tried to erase.
A stand of wild grain bends heavy with seed beside a shallow swale. Cutting it before the wind scatters the heads makes a simple meal.
Purple flowers mark a patch of wild onions under thin soil. Pulling them carefully keeps the bulbs whole.
White yarrow blooms across a low ridge where the grass grows short. The useful heads must be clipped before insects strip them.
A muddy stock pond flashes with bluegill beneath dragonflies. Casting from the firm bank avoids sinking knee-deep in churned mud.
A slow creek bend hides catfish under a cut bank. The water is shallow, but the bank crumbles when stepped on too hard.
Last night's rain filled a wheel-rut pool with trapped minnows from the flooded ditch. The opportunity will be gone by noon.
Sharp flint nodules poke through the soil on a low rise. Sorting good cores from cracked stone takes a practiced eye.
A creek has exposed clean clay below the grass roots. The bank is low but slick, and careless digging clouds the water.
Rust-colored water gathers where the plain dips into a marshy hollow. Iron nodules can be lifted from the muck before it swallows tools.
A cottonwood has fallen beside a seasonal creek, dry on top and damp below. The straight sections make light, workable timber.
An abandoned fence line sags across the grass, its posts silvered by weather but still solid. Pulling them cleanly saves hours of cutting.
Willows crowd a shallow wash where their flexible shoots can be cut for baskets and bindings. The best shoots grow over slick mud.
The grass parts only after the party rides between two marker stones. Crossbows rise from shallow pits and a calm voice demands the cargo.
Rows of wheat ripple against the wind, then sink as something burrows beneath them. The ankheg erupts in a spray of soil and roots.
A scarecrow turns its head after sunset though the wind has died. Straw spills from its sleeves like reaching fingers.
A wagon trail vanishes under tallgrass except for bends marked by half-buried stones. Reading the stones keeps wheels out of bad ground.
Wild horses break across the plain in a rolling wall of dust. Fast movement and steady nerves keep the party from being trampled.
A plow has struck an iron strongbox beneath a fallow strip. The lock is old, the coins older, and the field suddenly watched.
A drover's bell line lies cut beside hoofprints that turn sharply away from the road. The herd is moving too quietly for natural fear.
A hayrick smolders without flame near a small farmstead. Beneath it is a crawlspace, fresh bootprints, and a sealed message.
Two rival riders ask for witnesses at a standing stone circle before sunset. One rider carries a map meant for someone else.
A new sinkhole has opened in a barley field, revealing worked stone below. Farmhands hear chanting whenever they lower a lantern.
A Harper courier is hiding in grass taller than a horse, wounded and unwilling to trust strangers. Their message warns of a false toll on the road.
Wild oats ripple in a dry hollow just off the trail. Cutting the heads before the wind rises keeps most of the grain.
Sweet clover grows thick along a sunny ridge, buzzing with bees. The leaves dry well if gathered without crushing them.
Small perch gather where an irrigation ditch meets a slow creek. A quick net works before the water muddies.
Heavy carp nose through weed in a farm pond. They are easy to spot and hard to lift without breaking the line.
After rain, frogs crowd the flooded furrows. Catching enough for stew means moving before cranes arrive.
A cleared fieldstone pile hides dense blue-gray stones useful for foundations. Sorting them saves hauling useless rock.
A slick clay pan lies beneath thin grass near a seasonal pond. Clean clay must be cut before boots churn it dirty.
Bog iron in the low swale
Rusty nodules show where water sits in a low swale. The best bog iron lies just below the root mat.
A dead poplar in a farm windbreak has dried straight and pale. Cutting it risks spooking animals in the nearby field.
Broken wagon bows lie beside an old campsite, their ash wood still springy. The bends can be salvaged if unlashed carefully.
A stack of unused fence rails sits under oilcloth at an abandoned boundary. Some rails are warped, but many remain sound.
Wild sunflowers nod over a dry ditch, heavy with seeds. Birds have started the harvest, so quick hands save the best heads.
